content:
# Leviticus Chapter 12 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 64v–65r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_012_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_012_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 96–97 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_012_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_012_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 12 is the shortest chapter in Leviticus — only 8 verses — yet it introduces the second major topic of the purity legislation (chs. 11–15): bodily discharges, beginning with the blood of parturition. The chapter divides into three movements: (1) vv.1–4: the law for a son — 7 days impurity, circumcision on day 8, 33 days of blood-purification (dam toharah); (2) v.5: the law for a daughter — the doubled period, 14 days impurity + 66 days of blood-purification; (3) vv.6–8: the purification offerings — standard (lamb + pigeon) and the poor-person's substitution (two pigeons/doves). The chapter is addressed to Moshe alone (v.1), unlike the dual Moshe-Aharon address of ch.11. The connection to ch.15 (women's blood-discharges) is explicit via the term niddah (v.2). The connection to Gen 17 (circumcision) is equally explicit: the only mention of circumcision in all of Leviticus occurs here at v.3, embedding the Abrahamic covenant-sign within the purity legislation. The theological logic of the doubled periods for a daughter (v.5) is not stated in the text; it is one of the most debated cruxes of Leviticus. MT and English versification are identical throughout the chapter.]*

---

**1** And the LORD [YHWH] spoke [vayedabber] to Moshe [el-Moshe] saying [lemor] [vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor — "And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying"; this is the standard legislative introduction of Leviticus, used to open each new legal unit; the address here is to Moshe alone — not to Moshe and Aharon together as in 11:1; the purity legislation of chs. 11–15 alternates between sole-address-to-Moshe (ch.12: childbirth; ch.13–14: skin-disease, given to both) and dual-address; the LXX renders the opening identically: Καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγων (Kai elalēsen kyrios pros Mōysēn legōn), "And the Lord spoke to Moses, saying"]:

**2** "Speak to the sons of Yisra'el [dabber el-bney Yisra'el] saying [lemor]: A woman [ishah] who [ki] gives seed [tazria'] [ki tazria' — "when she gives seed"; tazria' is hiphil 3rd feminine singular of zara', "to sow seed"; in hiphil it means "to cause seed to be sown / to become pregnant / to conceive"; the word literally frames conception in agricultural/sowing imagery; the LXX renders tazria' as σπερματισθῇ (spermatisthē, "shall receive seed / shall be impregnated"), from σπέρμα (sperma, "seed") — recognizing the same root-sense; the hiphil form is unusual and has generated significant discussion: some argue tazria' describes the woman as the one who "sows" or "emits" seed — possibly an ancient observation of female reproductive fluid; others simply read it as "when she conceives"; the Samaritan Pentateuch and some Qumran texts agree with the MT form; the LXX's σπερματισθῇ is a passive ("is inseminated"), which shifts the agency slightly] and bears [ve-yaldah] [ve-yaldah — "and she bears / and she gives birth"; yaldah is qal perfect (used with vav-consecutive / perfective sense) of yalad, "to give birth"; this is the standard term for birth (cf. Gen 3:16; 4:1; 16:1); notably here the subject is feminine, so the participle/verb is feminine; the LXX renders ve-yaldah as καὶ τέκῃ (kai tekē, "and she gives birth"), from τίκτω (tiktō, "to bring forth / to give birth")] a male [zakhar] [zakhar — "a male / a male-child"; zakhar is the standard biblical term for the male sex; LXX: ἄρσεν (arsen, "male")] — she shall be unclean [ve-tame'ah] seven days [shivat yamim] [shivat yamim — "seven days"; the number seven structures the entire unit — 7 days initial impurity for a son, 33 days blood-purification (7 + 33 = 40 total before the offering), and for a daughter the periods double: 14 days + 66 days = 80 total; the heptadic structure is consistent throughout the Levitical purity legislation; the LXX preserves ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας (hepta hēmeras, "seven days")]; as in the days of the niddah [ki-yemey niddat devotah] [ki-yemey niddat devotah — "as in the days of the menstrual impurity of her infirmity / her weakness"; niddah is the technical term for menstrual impurity, from the root nadad ("to wander, to flee, to separate") — the niddah is one who is set apart from normal community life during her flow; devotah is from davah ("to be weak / to be sick / to be faint"), used specifically in the context of menstrual sickness/impurity (cf. Lev 15:33; 20:18; Isa 30:22); the phrase ki-yemey niddat devotah assimilates the postpartum impurity of childbirth to the regular menstrual-impurity cycle: the woman is impure after birth in the same way and degree as during her monthly niddah; this cross-reference explicitly links ch.12 to ch.15 (the discharge-laws), unifying the bodily-blood legislation; the LXX renders ki-yemey niddat devotah as κατὰ τὰς ἡμέρας τοῦ χωρισμοῦ τῆς ἀφέδρου αὐτῆς (kata tas hēmeras tou chorismou tēs aphédrou autēs, "according to the days of the separation of her menstruation"), using ἄφεδρος (aphedros, "menstrual flow / the one who sits apart") — a technical Greek medical term for menstruation, from ἀπό (apo, "from") + ἕδρα (hedra, "seat") — "the one seated apart / removed from her seat" — which well captures the niddah root-sense of separation] she shall be unclean [titmah] [titmah — qal imperfect 3rd feminine singular of tame', "to be/become unclean"; the imperfect here is fientive / durative: she is in an ongoing state of impurity for the seven-day period; the LXX renders titmah as ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται (akathartos estai, "she shall be unclean"), using the standard LXX equivalence ἀκάθαρτος for tame'].

**3** "And on the eighth day [u-va-yom ha-shemini] [u-va-yom ha-shemini — "and on the eighth day"; the eighth day in Leviticus is consistently the day of transition — the day of completion and new beginning: the eighth day of Aharon's inauguration (Lev 9:1); the eighth day for the presentation of certain firstborn animals (Lev 22:27); here the eighth day for circumcision; the number eight in the Levitical system signals the moment of crossing from one status to another — from the seven-day world of creational time into the realm of covenant-consecration; the LXX renders u-va-yom ha-shemini as τῇ δὲ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ὀγδόῃ (tē de hēmera tē ogdoē, "and on the eighth day")] the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised [yimmol besar orlato] [yimmol besar orlato — "the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised"; yimmol is niphal imperfect of mul, "to circumcise"; the niphal here is the passive-tolerative: "shall be circumcised / is to be circumcised"; besar ("flesh") + orlah ("foreskin") = "the flesh of his foreskin"; the circumcision command is the only direct mention of circumcision in the entire book of Leviticus — it appears here parenthetically within the childbirth-purity law, rather than as an independent command; its function is not to establish the circumcision rite (that was established in Gen 17:9-14) but to synchronize the purity law with the covenantal calendar: the child must be circumcised precisely on day 8, i.e., while the mother is still in her initial seven-day impurity; this means the circumcision takes place before the mother's full purification is complete — she cannot hold her son for his circumcision in a state of full ritual purity; the LXX renders yimmol besar orlato as περιτμηθήσεται τὸ παιδίον τὴν σάρκα τῆς ἀκροβυστίας αὐτοῦ (peritmēthēsetai to paidion tēn sarka tēs akrobystias autou, "the child shall be circumcised the flesh of its foreskin"), using περιτέμνω (peritemnō, "to circumcise around") and ἀκροβυστία (akrobystia, "foreskin") — a uniquely LXX-Greek term for foreskin, from ἄκρος (akros, "tip, extreme") + possibly a transliteration base; ἀκροβυστία is not found in pre-LXX Greek literature, suggesting it was coined by the Jewish translators; it becomes the standard NT term for the uncircumcised state (Rom 2:25-27; 4:10-12; Gal 5:6; 6:15; Col 3:11)].

**4** "And thirty days [u-sheloshim yom] and three days [vi-sheloshet yamim] [u-sheloshim yom vi-sheloshet yamim — "and thirty days and three days"; the MT construction is slightly unusual — the number is expressed as "thirty days and three days" rather than the more natural "thirty-three days" (shloshim u-sheloshah); this breaking of 33 into 30 + 3 is a Hebrew idiom for round-number-plus-remainder, common in biblical numerical expressions; the LXX renders it as τριάκοντα τρεῖς ἡμέρας (triakonta treis hēmeras, "thirty three days"), combining the two Hebrew phrases into one Greek phrase] she shall remain [teshev] [teshev — "she shall sit / she shall remain / she shall stay"; yashav in the imperfect: "she shall dwell, remain, sit"; the same root is used of sitting in the tent (Gen 18:1) and of dwelling in the land; here it conveys a sustained, settled period of waiting; the LXX renders teshev as μενεῖ (menei, "she shall remain"), from μένω (menō, "to stay, to remain") — precise equivalent] in blood-of-purification [bi-deme toharah] [bi-deme toharah — "in blood of purification"; dam toharah is one of the most distinctive expressions in the chapter; toharah means "purity / purification" (the noun from taher, "to be clean"); the phrase seems paradoxical: "blood of purity" — how can blood, which conveys impurity, be called "blood of purity"? Most commentators understand dam toharah as describing a transitional state: the postpartum blood that flows during the 33-day period is not the blood of menstrual impurity (niddah) — it does not render the woman impure to the same degree as the initial 7 days — but it is not yet the blood of a fully purified woman either; it is "purifying blood" — blood in the process of becoming-clean; the woman in this period may have normal marital relations (unlike during the niddah-period of v.2) but may not enter the sanctuary or touch holy things until the full 40 days (7 + 33) have elapsed; the LXX renders dam toharah as ἐν αἵματι ἀκαθαρσίας αὐτῆς (en haimati akatharsias autēs, "in the blood of her impurity") — a significant divergence from the MT; where the MT says "blood of purification/purity," the LXX says "blood of her impurity" (ἀκαθαρσία = impurity/uncleanness); this is one of the major LXX divergences in the chapter, and it flattens the theological nuance of the MT's transitional state; the LXX effectively extends the impurity-framing to cover the entire 40-day period, whereas the MT distinguishes the initial 7-day impurity from the subsequent 33-day dam-toharah period; she shall not touch [lo' tigg'a] any holy thing [be-khol-qodesh] [be-khol-qodesh — "in anything holy / any holy thing"; qodesh is the realm of the sacred — the sanctuary, its furnishings, the sacrificial offerings, the tithes; the prohibition is comprehensive: during the dam-toharah period the woman may not contact the sacred sphere; the prohibition is formulated negatively (lo' tigg'a, "she shall not touch") rather than as a declaration of her status (tame' hi', "she is impure"), which is consistent with the intermediate character of the dam-toharah state; the LXX: οὐχ ἅψεται παντὸς ἁγίου (ouch hapsetai pantos hagiou, "she shall not touch anything holy") — precise agreement with the MT] and to the sanctuary [ve-el-ha-miqdash] she shall not come [lo' tavo'] [lo' tavo' — "she shall not enter / she shall not come"; the prohibition of entering the miqdash (sanctuary) during this period echoes the exclusion of the impure from the sacred precinct throughout Leviticus (cf. 15:31; Num 5:2-3); the LXX renders lo' tavo' as οὐκ εἰσελεύσεται (ouk eiseleusetai, "she shall not enter"), from εἰσέρχομαι (eiserchomai, "to enter into")] until the days of her purification are complete [ad-meloet yemey toharah] [ad-meloet yemey toharah — "until the filling-up of the days of her purification"; meloet is the qal infinitive construct of male', "to be full / to fill"; the phrase ad-meloet = "until the completing/filling-up of"; yemey toharah = "the days of her purification/cleanness"; the same root appears at 8:33 (the "filling/completing" of the ordination-days of Aharon); the LXX renders ad-meloet yemey toharah as ἕως ἂν πληρωθῶσιν αἱ ἡμέραι καθάρσεως αὐτῆς (heōs an plērōthōsin hai hēmerai katharseōs autēs, "until the days of her purification are fulfilled"), using πληρόω (plēroō, "to fulfill, to fill up, to complete") — a theologically resonant term in the LXX and NT (cf. Luke 2:22: "when the days of their purification according to the law of Moses were fulfilled" — the same LXX language, applied to Miryam's postpartum purification after the birth of Yeshua)].

**5** "And if a female [ve-im-neqevah] she bears [teled] [ve-im-neqevah teled — "and if a female she bears"; neqevah is the standard term for the female sex — from naqav, "to pierce / to puncture"; the naming of the female body by a word meaning "the pierced one" or "the opening" reflects the anatomical directness of biblical Hebrew; LXX: θῆλυ (thēly, "female"), the standard Greek term], she shall be unclean [ve-tame'ah] two weeks [shevua'im] [shevua'im — "two weeks / two sevens"; the dual form of shavua' ("week / seven-day period"); for a daughter the initial impurity period is doubled — 14 days versus 7 days for a son; the text gives no reason for the doubling; ancient and modern commentators have proposed many explanations: (1) the daughter's own future capacity for menstrual impurity doubles the ritual concern; (2) the second-birth theory (the daughter will herself one day give birth, so as it were two births are being marked); (3) the doubling reflects a lesser ritual status for daughters; (4) the doubling may reflect ancient physiological beliefs about female births requiring more postpartum blood-flow; none of these explanations is stated in the text itself; the LXX renders shevua'im as δύο ἑβδομάδας (dyo hebdomadas, "two weeks")], as in her menstrual impurity [ke-niddata] [ke-niddata — "as in her niddah / as in her menstrual-separation state"; the same comparison to the regular menstrual cycle as in v.2, now applied to the 14-day period for a daughter; the LXX renders ke-niddata as κατὰ τὴν ἀφέδρου αὐτῆς (kata tēn aphedrou autēs, "according to her menstrual period")]; and sixty [u-shishim] days [yom] and six days [ve-sheshet yamim] [u-shishim yom ve-sheshet yamim — "and sixty days and six days"; again the broken construction "sixty days and six days" = 66 days, exactly double the 33 days of v.4; the LXX renders this as ἑξήκοντα ἓξ ἡμέρας (hexēkonta hex hēmeras, "sixty six days")] she shall remain [teshev] on blood-of-purification [al-deme toharah] [al-deme toharah — "on blood of purification"; the same phrase as in v.4 (bi-deme toharah), with the preposition changed from be- ("in") to al- ("on / upon / concerning"); this minor shift in preposition may reflect a slightly different sense — "sitting upon (the period of) blood-of-purification" versus "remaining in (the state of) blood-of-purification"; the difference is likely stylistic rather than substantive; the LXX renders al-deme toharah as ἐπὶ αἵματι ἀκαθαρσίας αὐτῆς (epi haimati akatharsias autēs, "upon the blood of her impurity") — again choosing ἀκαθαρσία ("impurity") where the MT has toharah ("purification/purity"), consistent with its rendering of v.4].

---

**6** "And when the days [u-vi-melo't yemey toharatah] of her purification are completed [li-ven o le-vat] [u-vi-melo't yemey toharatah li-ven o le-vat — "and at the completing of the days of her purification, for a son or for a daughter"; the phrase melo't yemey toharatah echoes the end of v.4 (ad-meloet yemey toharah), forming a bracket: the law of v.4 begins with "until the days of purification are complete" and v.6 opens with "at the completing of those days"; the phrase "for a son or for a daughter" (li-ven o le-vat) applies both cases (vv.2-5) to the same offering-requirement — the purification ritual is identical regardless of the sex of the child; the LXX renders u-vi-melo't yemey toharatah li-ven o le-vat as Καὶ ὅταν ἀναπληρωθῶσιν αἱ ἡμέραι καθάρσεως αὐτῆς ἐφ᾽ υἱῷ ἢ ἐφ᾽ θυγατρί (Kai hotan anaplērōthōsin hai hēmerai katharseōs autēs eph' hyiō ē eph' thygatri, "And when the days of her purification are fulfilled for a son or for a daughter") — faithful to the MT] — she shall bring [tavi'] a lamb [keves] in its first year [ben-shenato] [ben-shenato — "son of its year / in its first year"; ben-shenah / ben-shanah is the standard Levitical phrase for an animal less than one year old (cf. Lev 9:3; 23:12, 18, 19); the LXX renders ben-shenato as ἐνιαύσιον (eniaysion, "of a year / yearling"), from ἐνιαυτός (eniavtos, "year")] for a burnt-offering [le-olah] [le-olah — "for an olah [burnt-offering]"; the olah is the whole-burnt offering — entirely consumed on the altar, ascending (from 'alah, "to go up") to the LORD as an ascent-offering; the olah in this context is not for sin-atonement but for consecration and dedication — the woman is offering herself back to the full covenant life of Israel through this ascending-offering; the LXX renders le-olah as εἰς ὁλοκαύτωμα (eis holokautōma, "for a whole-burnt-offering"), from ὅλος (holos, "whole") + καίω (kaiō, "to burn") — a calque on the Hebrew 'alah-offering as "whole-burnt"], and a young-of-the-pigeons [u-ven-yonah] or a turtledove [o tor] [u-ven-yonah o tor — "and a young of the pigeon, or a turtledove"; yonah is the domestic pigeon (Columba livia); ben-yonah = "son of a pigeon / a young pigeon / a squab"; tor is the turtledove (Streptopelia turtur), the migratory dove of the Levant whose arrival signals spring (Song 2:12); the turtledove appears throughout Leviticus as a sacrificial bird for those of limited means; the LXX renders u-ven-yonah o tor as καὶ νεοσσὸν περιστερᾶς ἢ τρυγόνα (kai neosston peristerēs ē trygona, "and a young of a pigeon or a turtledove"), using νεοσσός (neossos, "chick / young bird") and τρυγών (trygōn, "turtledove") — precise equivalents] for a sin-offering [le-chattat] [le-chattat — "for a chattat [sin-offering]"; the chattat is the purification/sin offering whose blood is applied to the altar to purify a defiled person or object; in this context the chattat addresses the ritual impurity (tum'ah) of the postpartum state — the woman is not morally guilty of sin by giving birth; rather, the chattat ritually transitions her from the state of tum'ah back to the state of toharah; this is consistent with the use of chattat throughout chs. 11–15 for ritual rather than moral purification; the LXX renders le-chattat as περὶ ἁμαρτίας (peri hamartias, "concerning sin / for sin") — the standard LXX rendering of chattat as sin-related, which can mislead readers into thinking moral guilt is involved, but the forensic/ritual function is primary here] to the entrance [el-petach] of the Tent of Meeting [Ohel Mo'ed] [el-petach Ohel Mo'ed — "to the entrance of the Tent of Meeting"; Ohel Mo'ed is the Tabernacle — the designated site of all prescribed Israelite sacrifice and priestly mediation during the wilderness period; the offering must be brought to the Tent, to the priest; it cannot be a private act; the LXX renders Ohel Mo'ed as σκηνὴν τοῦ μαρτυρίου (skēnēn tou martyriou, "tent of the witness/testimony") — a translation that renders mo'ed ("meeting / appointed time") as martyrion ("testimony / witness"), which departs from the more obvious "tent of meeting" (σκηνὴν τοῦ μαρτυρίου occurs throughout the LXX as the fixed equivalent of Ohel Mo'ed)] to the priest [el-ha-kohen]:

**7** "And he shall bring it near [ve-hiqrivo] [ve-hiqrivo — "and he shall offer it / cause it to draw near"; qorev in hiphil = "to bring near / to present as an offering"; the verb qarav is the root of qorban ("offering"), which simply means "the thing brought near [to God]"]; before the LORD [lifney YHWH] [lifney YHWH — "before the face of the LORD / in the presence of the LORD"; the priestly presentation is simultaneously a presentation before the human priest and before the divine LORD — the Tent is the site of divine dwelling; the LXX renders lifney YHWH as ἔναντι κυρίου (enanti kyriou, "before the Lord")] and he shall make atonement for her [ve-khipper aleha] [ve-khipper aleha — "and he shall cover for her / he shall atone for her"; kipper (piel of kapar, "to cover / to wipe / to atone") is the central priestly act of the sacrificial system — the priest applies the blood of the chattat to the altar-horns, covering/wiping away the ritual impurity that separates the woman from the sanctuary; aleha = "for her / on her behalf"; the LXX renders ve-khipper aleha as καὶ ἐξιλάσεται περὶ αὐτῆς (kai exilāsetai peri autēs, "and he shall make atonement/propitiation concerning her"), using ἐξιλάσκομαι (exilaskomai, "to propitiate / to make atonement"), which in the LXX consistently represents kipper and its derivatives; the LXX's exilaskomai and the NT's cognate ἱλαστήριον (hilastērion, "mercy-seat / propitiation" — Rom 3:25; Heb 9:5) share this root]; and she shall be clean [ve-taherah] [ve-taherah — "and she shall be clean / and she is made clean"; taherah is qal perfect of taher, "to be clean / to be pure"; with the vav-consecutive it is translated as a result or future: "and [then] she shall be clean"; the act of priestly atonement (kipper) produces the state of ritual purity (toharah) — the ritual sequence is: dam toharah period → offering at the Tent → priestly atonement → restored purity; the LXX renders ve-taherah as καὶ καθαρισθήσεται (kai katharisthēsetai, "and she shall be purified"), using καθαρίζω (katharizō, "to cleanse, to purify") in the passive — implying that the purification is done to her by the priestly-divine action, not by herself] from the fountain of her blood [mi-meqor dameha] [mi-meqor dameha — "from the source/spring of her blood"; meqor is "spring, source, fountain" (from qur, "to dig"); it is used of a natural spring or water-source, but also specifically of female genital blood: Lev 20:18 uses meqor for the source of the woman's menstrual blood; Ps 68:26 uses it for "springs of water"; the phrase mi-meqor dameha gives the theological-anatomical basis of the purification: it is from the fountain-source of her blood that she has been made clean; the LXX renders mi-meqor dameha as ἀπὸ τῆς πηγῆς τοῦ αἵματος αὐτῆς (apo tēs pēgēs tou haimatos autēs, "from the spring/fountain of her blood"), using πηγή (pēgē, "spring, fountain") — the same word used in Mark 5:29 for the "fountain of blood" that dried up when Yeshua healed the woman with the issue of blood, a deliberate echo of this Levitical vocabulary]. This is the Torah [zot torat] [zot torat — "this is the instruction/law of"; Torah here in its basic sense of "instruction, teaching, ruling" — the divine legislative directive; LXX: οὗτος ὁ νόμος (houtos ho nomos, "this is the law")] of the one who gives birth [ha-yoledet] [ha-yoledet — "the one who gives birth / the woman-in-labor"; yoledet is the qal participle feminine of yalad, "to give birth" — used as a substantive: "the birthing-one / the parturient woman"; the LXX renders ha-yoledet as τῆς τεκούσης (tēs tekousēs, "of the one who gave birth"), using the aorist participle of τίκτω (tiktō)] for a male or for a female [la-zakhar o la-neqevah] [la-zakhar o la-neqevah — "for a male or for a female"; the closing summary confirms that the Torah of vv.2–7 covers both cases; the LXX renders la-zakhar o la-neqevah as τὸ ἄρσεν ἢ τὸ θῆλυ (to arsen ē to thēly, "the male or the female")].

**8** "And if her hand [ve-im lo timtsa yadah] [ve-im lo timtsa yadah — "and if her hand does not find / and if she cannot afford"; the Hebrew idiom lo timtsa yadah literally means "her hand does not find" — i.e., her resources are insufficient; the "hand" (yad) represents capability, capacity, means; the construction timtsa yadah ("her hand finds") is an idiom for "she has the means / she can afford" (cf. Lev 5:7; 14:21, 22, 30, 31, 32; 25:26, 28, 47, 49; Num 6:21); the negation lo timtsa yadah = "if she cannot afford / if she lacks the means"; this is the provision for economic insufficiency — the poor-person's offering; the LXX renders ve-im lo timtsa yadah as ἐὰν δὲ μὴ εὑρίσκῃ ἡ χεὶρ αὐτῆς (ean de mē heuriskē hē cheir autēs, "and if her hand does not find"), preserving the idiomatic literalism] does not find [sufficient] for a lamb [day seh] [day seh — "the sufficiency of a sheep/lamb"; day is "sufficiency / enough"; seh is a sheep or goat of unspecified age (as distinct from keves, "lamb," or 'ez, "she-goat"); the phrase day seh = "the means for a lamb / enough [to afford] a sheep"; the LXX renders day seh as ἱκανὸν εἰς ἀμνόν (hikanon eis amnon, "sufficient for a lamb"), using ἱκανός (hikanos, "sufficient, enough") and ἀμνός (amnos, "lamb") — the word used of the sacrificial lamb throughout the LXX and notably in John 1:29 ("the lamb of God"), Isa 53:7, and Rev 5:6; the poor-person's option reduces the lamb-requirement to a smaller bird-offering without diminishing the atoning efficacy] — she shall take [ve-laqqechah] two turtledoves [shtey torim] [shtey torim — "two turtledoves"; turtledoves appear throughout the sacrificial legislation as the standard bird offering (Lev 1:14; 5:7, 11; 14:22; 15:14, 29; Num 6:10); two birds are required because the standard offering of v.6 has two components (a lamb for the olah + one bird for the chattat); the poor-person's substitution replaces the lamb with a second bird, providing one bird for each component; LXX: δύο τρυγόνας (dyo trygōnas, "two turtledoves")] or two young-of-the-pigeons [o shtey bney yonah] [o shtey bney yonah — "or two young of the pigeons / two pigeon-chicks"; the alternative to the turtledove option — either two turtledoves or two young pigeons are acceptable; the LXX renders o shtey bney yonah as ἢ δύο νεοσσοὺς περιστερῶν (ē dyo neossous peristerōn, "or two young pigeons / two pigeon-chicks") — Luke 2:24 cites this exact provision (in LXX language: "a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons") when describing Miryam's offering at the Temple after Yeshua's birth, explicitly linking the poverty-offering of Lev 12:8 to the incarnation narrative — the mother of the Messiah was too poor to afford a lamb, and brought the bird-substitution; this gospel echo is one of the most direct LXX-Leviticus allusions in the NT], one [ha-echad] for a burnt-offering [le-olah] and one [ve-ha-echad] for a sin-offering [le-chattat] [ha-echad le-olah ve-ha-echad le-chattat — "the one for an olah and the one for a chattat"; the assignment of the two birds to their respective sacrificial functions is explicit: one bird ascends whole as the olah (consecration/dedication), the other's blood is applied as the chattat (purification/atonement); the LXX renders these phrases identically: τὸ ἓν εἰς ὁλοκαύτωμα καὶ τὸ ἓν περὶ ἁμαρτίας (to hen eis holokautōma kai to hen peri hamartias, "the one for a whole-burnt-offering and the one for sin")]; and the priest shall make atonement for her [ve-khipper aleha ha-kohen] [ve-khipper aleha ha-kohen — "and the priest shall atone for her / cover for her"; repeating the kipper formula of v.7, confirming that the poor-person's offering has the same atoning efficacy as the standard offering — no lesser grace is available to the poor woman; the LXX renders ve-khipper aleha ha-kohen as καὶ ἐξιλάσεται περὶ αὐτῆς ὁ ἱερεύς (kai exilāsetai peri autēs ho hiereus, "and the priest shall make atonement concerning her")] and she shall be clean [ve-taherah] [ve-taherah — "and she shall be clean"; the same closing formula as v.7; the chapter ends where v.7 ends, with the declaration of purity restored; the LXX: καὶ καθαρισθήσεται (kai katharisthēsetai, "and she shall be purified")]."

---

## Textual Notes

**On niddah (v.2):** The term niddah [נִדָּה] derives from the root nadad (נָדַד), "to wander, to flee, to be separated." The niddah is literally "she who is set apart / she who has wandered from [normal life]." The LXX consistently renders niddah as ἄφεδρος (aphedros, "the one who sits apart from [her seat]"), which captures the separation-sense from a different angle. The MT's linking of postpartum blood to the niddah-cycle (vv.2, 5) is a deliberate cross-reference to ch.15, framing childbirth within the broader category of genital-blood discharges.

**On dam toharah (vv.4, 5):** The phrase dam toharah [דַּם טָהֳרָה, "blood of purification/purity"] is a theologically compressed expression for the intermediate status of postpartum blood after the initial niddah-period has ended. It is blood that is in the process of becoming-clean — not yet the blood of a fully pure woman, but no longer the impurity-conveying blood of the niddah. The LXX's rendering as ἐν αἵματι ἀκαθαρσίας αὐτῆς ("in the blood of her impurity") at v.4 and ἐπὶ αἵματι ἀκαθαρσίας αὐτῆς at v.5 collapses this theological distinction, treating the entire 33/66-day period as impure. This is a genuine interpretive divergence between the MT and LXX traditions.

**On the doubled periods for a daughter (v.5):** The text doubles every number: 14 days (vs. 7) and 66 days (vs. 33). No rationale is given. The doubled-period has generated extensive commentary from the Talmud (b.Niddah 31b proposes that a son forms earlier and the body recovers faster; a daughter "doubles" the woman's blood loss) to Maimonides (who saw the doubling as a reflection on the greater postpartum blood-flow associated with female births in ancient medicine) to modern anthropological readings (menarcheal foreshadowing for the daughter). The MT is silent; the LXX also offers no explanation; the text presents the law without justification.

**On kipper (vv.7–8):** The repeated use of ve-khipper aleha (vv.7, 8) — "he shall atone for her / cover for her" — is crucial for understanding what the chattat-sacrifice accomplishes in this context. The woman has not sinned by giving birth. The kipper-act removes a ritual state (tum'ah) rather than a moral condition (guilt). This distinction is essential: the postpartum offering does not presuppose that childbirth is sinful, but that the bodily process of birth creates a liminal ritual condition that must be formally resolved before the woman can re-enter the sanctuary and its associated covenant life.

**On Luke 2:24 and the poor-person's offering (v.8):** The evangelist's note that Miryam brought "a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons" (Luke 2:24, citing Lev 12:8 in LXX language) is not incidental background detail — it is a deliberate theological marker identifying the family of Yeshua as unable to afford the standard lamb. The Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world (John 1:29, again ἀμνός — the same LXX sacrificial term) was himself born to a mother who could only afford the bird-substitution. The inversion is stark and presumably intentional: the lamb is present in the story, but as the child, not the offering.

**On circumcision in Leviticus (v.3):** The hapax character of circumcision in Leviticus is significant. The rite belongs to the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 17), not to the Sinai legislation proper — Leviticus does not re-establish or explain circumcision; it assumes it and synchronizes the purity-clock to it. The fact that the circumcision falls on day 8 — while the mother is still in her initial seven-day niddah — means that the covenant-sign is embedded in a moment of maternal ritual impurity. No provision is made to resolve this tension; the text simply juxtaposes them.
